questions.scenario:
A customer opens `https://orders.devpolaris.com`. The intended path is:

Route 53 hosted zone: devpolaris.com
Alias target: dualstack.devpolaris-orders-alb-123.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com
ALB listener: HTTPS 443
Target group: devpolaris-orders-api-tg

What does DNS prove in this path?

options.text: It tells the browser which entry point the friendly name should reach.
It proves the ALB certificate is trusted for `orders.devpolaris.com`.
It proves the target group has healthy ECS tasks.
It grants the ECS task role permission to call AWS APIs.

questions.explanation: DNS answers the location question: where should this name go? TLS answers the identity and trust question, and target health comes later in the path. Keeping those promises separate makes custom-domain debugging much easier.

questions.scenario: The team wants to replace the ALB behind devpolaris-orders-api without asking customers to change bookmarks. Which design supports that?

options.text: Keep `orders.devpolaris.com` stable and update the Route 53 alias to the new ALB when it is ready.
Tell customers to use the generated ALB DNS name because it is the real entry point.
Put the ECS task private IPs directly into public DNS so the ALB can be removed.
Issue a certificate for the old ALB DNS name and leave Route 53 unchanged.

questions.explanation: The friendly name gives users a stable URL while AWS resources behind it can change carefully. A Route 53 alias can point that name at the intended ALB. Customers should not need to learn the generated ALB DNS name.

questions.scenario: Which checks belong before blaming the Node backend when `orders.devpolaris.com` fails?

options.text: Does the public DNS name resolve to the expected ALB entry point?
Does the TLS certificate cover `orders.devpolaris.com`?
Does the HTTPS listener forward to the orders API target group?
Does the database security group allow browser IPs to connect to Postgres?

questions.explanation: A custom domain can fail at DNS, TLS, or listener routing before the backend code sees a request. Those layers are the first promises to prove. Browsers should not connect to the database.

questions.scenario: `dig +short orders.devpolaris.com` returns an empty result from a public network. The ECS service has two healthy targets. What should the team inspect first?

options.text: The public hosted zone, the `orders` record, and domain delegation to Route 53 name servers.
The app `/health` endpoint, because healthy targets can cause DNS records to disappear.
The database security group, because DNS queries use TCP 5432.
The task IAM role, because `dig` requires `route53:ResolveName` from ECS.

questions.explanation: An empty public DNS answer means the name promise failed before the request can reach the ALB or backend. Healthy targets are useful later, but they cannot fix a missing public DNS record or broken delegation. Start at Route 53 and registrar delegation.

questions.scenario:
DNS resolves and TLS succeeds, but the ALB listener table says:

HTTPS:443 certificate orders.devpolaris.com default action fixed-response 404
HTTP:80 redirect to HTTPS:443

What is missing for the orders API path?

options.text: A listener action or rule that forwards the matching host to `devpolaris-orders-api-tg`.
A public IP on each ECS task so Route 53 can skip the ALB.
A DNS validation CNAME, because issued certificates cannot be used by listeners.
A database subnet group in public subnets so the listener can query orders.

questions.explanation: Once DNS and TLS work, the listener still needs to send the request somewhere useful. A fixed 404 default action does not forward to the app target group. The missing promise is listener routing, not task public IPs or database placement.
